g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Methods for Handling Deadlocks. We'll explore four key strategies operating systems use to manage deadlock situations.
A deadlock occurs when processes are permanently blocked, each holding resources another needs. This halts the entire system.
g.text: A state where a set of processes are permanently blocked because each process
is holding a resource and waiting for another resource held by another process.

g.data-narration: Four conditions must all hold simultaneously for a deadlock: Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, and Circular Wait.
Deadlock Prevention ensures at least one condition is never satisfied, thus eliminating the possibility of deadlock from the start.
g.text: Design the system so that at least one of the four conditions is NEVER satisfied.
This guarantees no deadlock can ever occur, but restricts resource usage.

g.data-narration: Four prevention techniques target each condition: eliminate mutual exclusion for shareable resources, require all resources upfront, allow preemption, and impose resource ordering.
Deadlock Avoidance uses the Banker's Algorithm to check if granting a request leads to a safe state before allocation.
g.text: Requires a priori knowledge of maximum resources each process may request.
Uses Banker's Algorithm to ensure the system NEVER enters an unsafe state.

g.data-narration: Banker's Algorithm uses four key data structures: Available, Maximum, Allocation, and Need matrices to track resource states.
In this example, with 12 tape drives and processes P0, P1, P2, the system is safe because P1's needs can be met first.
Detection allows deadlocks to occur, then periodically runs an algorithm to detect them. Recovery then breaks the deadlock.
g.text: Allow deadlocks to occur. Periodically run detection algorithm to identify deadlocks.
When detected, use recovery techniques to break the deadlock.

g.data-narration: Recovery can terminate all processes or one at a time. Alternatively, preempt resources from a victim process and rollback to a safe state.
g.text: Abort ALL or ONE at a time

g.data-narration: Most OS use the Ostrich Algorithm - ignoring deadlocks entirely. The rationale: deadlocks are rare, and the overhead of prevention is too costly.
g.text: The OS pretends deadlocks never occur. Most general-purpose OS use this approach.
Rationale: Deadlocks are rare; overhead of other methods not justified.

g.data-narration: Prevention guarantees no deadlock but has low utilization. Avoidance needs future knowledge. Detection allows occurrence but has recovery overhead. Ignorance is simplest but requires manual intervention.
In conclusion, modern OS typically use deadlock ignorance for applications while applying stricter methods in critical kernel subsystems for system reliability.
g.text: Most modern OS combine methods: ignorance for applications, strict prevention in kernel subsystems.
